EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mark Panthen, 36, of Bellingham, died Sunday afternoon after two avalanches on the north slope of the mountain, next to the Mount Baker Ski Resort. ||A man who was skiing with Panthen called an employee of the resort from a cellphone around 12:45 p.m., saying Panthen was injured and needed help.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mark Panthen, 36, of Bellingham, died Sunday afternoon after two avalanches on the north slope of the mountain, next to the Mount Baker Ski Resort.|A man who was skiing with Panthen called an employee of the resort from a cellphone around 12:45 p.m., saying Panthen was injured and needed help.|There were two avalanches within 15 minutes. The avalanches were at 4,200 feet.|Using a helicopter, emergency responders confirmed Panthen died around 2:20 p.m., authorities said. They provided aid to the other skier, who suffered a head injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet thunderstorms moving to the north and northeast at 20 mph produced heavy rainfall over parts of the East Slopes and North Cascades including parts of the Okanogan Valley. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A woman presumably drowned while swimming along the coast in Long Beach, WA. The US Coast Guard called off the search after 24 hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds through the Columbia River Gorge maintained below freezing surface temperatures for the Columbia Gorge, and areas of Clark County as a couple of precipitating events moved over the area Jan 3 and Jan 4. This resulted transportation challenges and school closures. There were a lot of car accidents and minor injuries from slips and falls on the ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 3 inches of snow fell follwed by a trace to 0.10 inch of freezing rain. Slick roads resulted in traffic delays and accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hiker, 65 year old Doug Walker, was found dead near Granite Mtn  in the Snoqualmie Pass area.  He was killed by avalanche.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 65-year old outdoors-man was found dead Friday morning on Granite Mountain after an all-night search. He had been snowshoeing and hiking with friends Thursday when the wind picked up. His companions decided to turn back and wait for Mr. Walker, who continued climbing a route that friends said he had traversed at least 200 times. He was likely caught in an avalanche, according to the King County Sheriff���s Office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: He was found where alpinist Monique Richard, 41 of Canada, described last seeing her climbing partner Arvid Lathi, 58 of Norway, in the area of the Gibraltar Ledges route.|They began their climb on Saturday morning, March 26. That afternoon Richard was seen descending from the Gibraltar Ledges route to Camp Muir. Several individuals assisted her to the public shelter at Camp Muir. On Monday she was airlifted to a local hospital. |An unresponsive climber was located in the area of the ledges by air reconnaissance on Monday, March 28. On the climbing team's descent from the summit on Saturday, March 26, they were caught out overnight in a winter storm, causing them to take shelter from extreme wind, blowing snow, and frigid temperatures on the lee side of a ridge. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: About 250,000 people lost power.  A 42 year old man died when his car was hit by a tree in Seattle's Seward Park.  Several homes were damaged.  Scaffolding at the UW was reduced to a pile of rubble by the winds.  The Hwy 520 bridge and Hood Canal Bridge were closed for several hours, as was parts of I-405.  There was minor damage to the 520 bridge draw span.  A semi truck was toppled on the Tacoma Narrows bridge, halting traffic.  Downed trees blocked two lanes of southbound 405 in Snohomish County.  Washington State Ferries canceled or delayed several routes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Destruction Island recorded 50+ KT winds for a few hours.  This verifies high wind on the north coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly short-lived thunderstorm intensified quickly and spun up a brief EF0 tornado that quickly dissipated.  Very heavy rain and some localized flash flooding occurred in downtown Kennewick with this storm.  Some reports of near the proximity of the tornado were: trees down and 0.75 inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: We received a cell phone video captured from the general public that showed swirling motion at the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a period of unseasonably warm weather, 17-year-old Jeramiah Smith swimming across the Green River in Auburn with six friends had trouble and then went under in the cold river water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During a period of unseasonably warm weather with Puget Sound area temperatures near 90 degrees, officials say 17-year-old Jeramiah Smith was swimming across the Green River in Auburn with six friends about 7:30 p.m. when he started having trouble about halfway through.  Smith���s friends said they saw him travel downstream and then go under water. River temperatures were near 50 degrees and cold water immersion was thought to be the cause of the youth's trouble in the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A young girl playing on the beach was swept into deep water likely by a rip current and drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A young girl was playing on the beach and ended up in deep water. No one witnessed how the girl was swept into the ocean, but it is most likely due to a rip current. She was found 500 feet from the shore and was in the water for around 30 minutes.

A number of thunderstorms producing intense rain on the Lime Belt burn scar area resulted in a debris flow. The heavy rain washed mud, large rock, and other debris down a roadside ditch and over the roadway into a field at Johnson Creek which is about 6 miles southeast of Conconully, WA. It also covered area with small hail. It was reported that estimated rainfall rates approached over one inch in 25 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of thunderstorms producing intense rain on the Lime Belt burn scar area resulted in a debris flow. The heavy rain washed mud, large rock, and other debris down a roadside ditch and over the roadway into field at Johnson Creek which is about 6 miles southeast of Conconully, WA. It also covered area with small hail. It was reported that estimated rainfall rates approached over one inch in 25 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong fast moving cold front brought severe thunderstorms to Extreme Eastern Washington during the afternoon and early evening hours of June 8,2016. The storms produced large hail and damaging wind.The most impressive storm spotter report noted 1.25 inch size hail causing dents in a car at Anatone, Washington. Not much further away in North Idaho the most impressive storm reports included 1.75 inch size hail at Craigmont with numerous 1.00 inch size hail reports near the towns of Winchester and Avery along with several 50 foot cottonwood trees blown over not far from Santa, Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported 1.25 inch size hail which caused dents in a car near Anatone, Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable warm southerly flow of moisture allowed thunderstorms to develop that moved rather quickly to the north at 25 mph. A few of the thunderstorms downdrafts resulted in power lines and trees down in the Spokane Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported power lines down near Sharp and Atlantic in downtown Spokane, Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms rotating around a large area of low pressure over the Pacific Northwest caused some flash flooding issues. Incidents of note include water over roadways and entering some basements of homes in the Nine Mile Falls area of Spokane as well as water and debris flowing across 291 between Francis Boulevard and Rifle Club Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported water over roadways and entering basements of some homes in and near Nine Mile Falls, WA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall over parts of the East Slopes and North Cascades including parts of the Okanogan Valley. Rainfall reports of over one inch in 40 minutes were picked up by some of the automated equipment near Okanogan, WA. The resulting heavy rainfall produced some debris flows and Flash floods near Chelan and Okanogan, WA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from Pole Pick Mountain, located 4 miles southwest of Loup Loup Campground which is also near Twisp, WA reported just over one inch of rainfall in 40 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet thunderstorms moving to the north and northeast at 20 mph produced heavy rainfall over parts of the East Slopes and North Cascades including parts of the Okanogan Valley. The resulting heavy rainfall produced some debris flows and Flash floods near Methow, Brewster and Carlton in Okanogan County of Washington. Later on in the day this same disturbance produced more severe thunderstorms with two tornadoes occurring, one near Mould, WA and a second near Airway Heights, WA along with a number of fallen trees in the Spokane area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported SR-153, Just north of mile post 26 is closed due to mud and debris on roadway which is approximately 4 miles north northeast of Carlton, WA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet thunderstorms moving to the north and northeast at 20 mph produced heavy rainfall over parts of the East Slopes and North Cascades including parts of the Okanogan Valley. The resulting heavy rainfall produced some debris flows and Flash floods near Methow, Brewster and Carlton in Okanogan County of Washington. Later on in the day this same disturbance produced more severe thunderstorms with two tornadoes occurring, one near Mould, WA and a second near Airway Heights, WA along with a number of fallen trees in the Spokane area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported tree blown down on Euclid road which is approximately 3 miles north of Airway Heights, WA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of slow moving and very wet thunderstorms moved through Eastern Washington. The thunderstorms that occurred in the afternoon and evening had enough strength and rainfall intensity to down some trees and produce some localized flooding in the Winthrop, Washington area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forest Service reported that heavy rain from thunderstorms resulted in a road washout on Ramsey Creek Road. A one mile stretch was blocked with mud and debris. The time of the event was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of slow moving and very wet thunderstorms moved through Eastern Washington. The thunderstorms that occurred in the afternoon and evening had enough strength and rainfall intensity to down some trees and produce some localized flooding in the Winthrop, Washington area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported that a thunderstorm brought down trees and powerlines between Addy and Chewelah, WA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Sunland fire, a human caused wildfire, grew to 1,912 acres in Grant County near the Gorge Amphitheater. Sheriff's deputies determined boaters sparked the fire when they came onshore to have lunch. Their campfire near the Columbia River spread into some brush and got out of control when the winds picked up.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No one was injured and no homes were lost, so sheriff���s deputies did not expect to file any charges against the boaters who started this wildfire. Agency reports note cost of $75,000 associated with suppression.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Timm Ranch Fire, located 12 miles east of Bridgeport, WA burned 1,358 acres in Okanogan county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official federal agency reports note that the Timm Ranch fire was human caused. Costs of suppression estimated at $30,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cayuse Mountain fire , a human caused wildfire initially located 7 miles southwest of Wellpinit, WA, grew to 18,116 acres before it was noted as contained on September 7,2016. The fire stated when the Hart fire (which stated the same day on August 21,2016 near Davenport, WA) jumped across the Spokane River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated costs associated with mitigation/suppression of the Cayuse Mountain fire was 3.8 million dollars. It was noted that 27 homes were lost between the two fires (the Cayuse fire and the Hart fire).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Buck Creek Fire was a lightning cause fire in Chelan county with a noted locations of 12 miles northwest of Trinity, WA. This fire grew to 3,500 acres (as the most recent fire report of October 6th reported). This fire was controlled in the late summer but has yet to be reported as contained. The expectation is that winter snowfall will put it out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The latest fire report of October 6th noted costs associated with fire suppression/mitigation of the Buck Creek Fire to be $640,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Antilon Lake fire, a human caused fire, burned 315 acres. Initially located 10 miles northest of Chelan, WA it was started by an equipment failure around 8 pm on Friday July 29,2016. That investigation is ongoing. Much of the area previously burned in the 2002 Deer Point Fire. Those previously burned areas were helpful to building containment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No injuries were reported and no structures were damaged. The noted costs associated with suppression/containment efforts of the Antilon Lake fire was $600,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Black Rock Road Fire, initially located 18 miles west of Odessa, WA, was a human caused fire. This fire burned 16,025 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No residences were reported lost but some outbuildings had burned and there were livestock losses reported. Latest official reports note costs associated with suppression efforts associated with the Black Rock Road fire were $450,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Elmer City Fire, initially located 1 mile North of Coulee Dam, WA, was a human caused fire. This fire burned 5,237 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elmer City Fire burned several power poles, knocking out electric power to a few customers of Nespelem Valley Electric. The cause of the fire is still under investigation, but a power line slap (one wire touching the other due to gusty winds) is being looked at, according to officials. Latest official reports noted costs associated with suppression efforts associated with the Elmer City Fire were $600,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Saul Fire is believed to have been started from the same storm system that ignited the Buck Creek Fire (by lightning on July 22,2016), but the Saul Fire was not reported until almost a month later. The difference between how these two fires burned makes for a great example as to how fire spreads in environments that have, or have not, seen recent fire activity. It is believed that the Buck Creek Fire area had not experienced any recent fire activity over the last couple centuries, while the Saul Fire area had seen fire activity over the last couple of decades. The Saul Fire grew to 550 acres (as the most recent fire report of October 6th notes). This fire has yet to be reported as extinguished but is considered inactive (Having likely been controlled sometime during the late summer). The expectation is that winter snowfall will put it out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated costs associated with mitigation/suppression of the Saul Fire was $7,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Spokane Complex consisted of two fires. The Wellesley Fire located near the northeast edge of the Northwood neighborhood in North Spokane, WA and the Yale Road fire located south of Spokane and Interstate 90, and southwest of the small community of Valleyford, WA. Both fires started on the afternoon of Sunday, August 21, 2016 as a dry cold front moved through the area with strong gusty winds. As a result, both fires made very active wind driven runs during their initial runs on August 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wellesley Fire was estimated to be 341 acres in size with two residences destroyed. |The Yale Road Fire was estimated to be 5,854 acres in size with  ten residences destroyed. Estimated costs associated with suppression/mitigation were 4.8 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Deep North Fire, initially located 25 miles northeast of Colville, WA, was a human caused wildfire. The size of the fire was noted to be 596 acres at containment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Costs associated with suppression/mitigation of the Deep North Fire were noted to be 5.8 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pickens North Fire, initially reported at 7 miles northeast of Tonasket, WA was a human caused wilfire. It grew to 760 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated costs associated with mitigation/suppression of the Pickens North Fire were $280,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures around 100 degrees lead to people seeking relief at local rivers. One drowning was reported at Paradise Point State Park west of La Center during this hot spell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Range 12 Fire started on Saturday evening around 7:00pm, July 30, 2016. The fire moved from the Yakima Training Center onto BLM, state and private lands. Wind driven fire quickly grew into sage and grass. The fire is currently (Aug 5th) 95% contained, but has covered over 176,600 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fire started on August 2 at 12:50pm and quickly grew due to high winds and dry conditions. It started in Garfield County, across from Granite Point, on the Snake River. The fire jumped the river to Whitman County at about 4:30pm. The fire was fought on both sides of the river; Garfield county to the south, and Whitman county to the north. The fire was approximately 10 miles northeast of Pomeroy, WA, 10 miles directly south of Pullman, and 24 miles west of Clarkston. The Snake River Fire burned 11,453 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Costs associated with suppression/mitigation of the Snake River fire were noted to be 1.6 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Hart fire, a human caused wildfire, initially located 13 miles north of Davenport, WA grew to 4,983 acres and was noted to be contained on August 24,2016. This fire jumped the Spokane river and started the Cayuse Mountain fire on the Spokane Indian Reservation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated costs associated with mitigation/suppression of the Hart fire was 2.9 million dollars. The Hart fire destroyed 26 outbuildings and damaged 13 more. It was noted in news stories from the region that 27 homes were lost between the two fires (the Cayuse fire and the Hart fire).

CZ_NAME: EASTERN STRAIT OF JUAN DE FUCA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly low level flow following a modified arctic front brought snow to the north side of the Olympic Peninsula along the Eastern Strait of Juan de Fuca.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six CoCoRaHS stations had verifying snowfall reports ranging from 4.5 to 8.3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough and a cold low level air mass brought heavy snow to the east Puget Sound lowlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warmer low pressure system moved into to Southwest Washington, bringing high winds along the South Washington Coast. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced a swath of heavy snow across the upper Columbia Basin of Eastern Washington. Five to 8 inches of snow was observed over the rising terrain of the eastern basin, with generally lower amounts in the 3 to 5 inch range to the west in the deep basin near the Cascade Mountains, and 2 to 3 inches to the east over the Palouse region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Observer in Ritzville measured 5.7 inches of snow accumulation overnight December 5th to the early morning of December 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and vigorous winter storm brought heavy snow to the mountains and valleys of eastern Washington and the eastern Columbia Basin. In addition to widespread mountain accumulations of 12 to 14 inches and valley and eastern basin accumulations of 3 to 6 inches, windy conditions after the snow ended produced areas of blowing snow closing roads and crippling traffic in near Blizzard conditions on the Washington Palouse and the Camas Prairie area of north Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Holden Village recorded 17.1 inches of new snow which fell through the day December 27th and continued until the morning of December 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system brought heavy snow to the Olympics and North Cascades. For all but the Stevens Pass area, this was the first winter storm of the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Waterhole SNOTEL measured 13 inches snow in the 12 hours ending 1 am Thu Nov 24 and 28 inches total for the warning period.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system brought heavy snow to the Olympics and North Cascades. For all but the Stevens Pass area, this was the first winter storm of the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mt Baker NWAC site measured 13 inches snow in the 12 hours ending 7 AM Thu Nov 24 and 41 inches total from 7 PM Wed Nov 23 to 4 AM Fri Nov 25.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system brought heavy snow to the Olympics and North Cascades. For all but the Stevens Pass area, this was the first winter storm of the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stevens Pass and Alpental NWAC sites measured 6 inches snow during the warning period, and Snoqualmie Pass NWAC site measured 4 inches snow during the warning period.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system brought heavy snow to the Olympics and North Cascades. For all but the Stevens Pass area, this was the first winter storm of the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Paradise NWAC site measured 15 inches snow during the warning period. Crystal NWAC site had 11 inches snow, and White Pass NWAC site had 6 inches snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving southeastward onto the Coast brought high winds to the South Washington Coast and Coastal Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station at Cape Disappointment recorded wind gusts up to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving southeastward onto the Coast brought high winds to the South Washington Coast and Coastal Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Abernathy RAWS site recorded wind gusts up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of disturbances under west-northwesterly flow brought several rounds of snow showers to the South Washington Cascades, totaling 8-14 inches at most of the SNOTEL sites. Winds gusting up to 55 mph also caused blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most SNOTELs reported 8-14. Potato Hill SNOTEL at 4510 feet recorded 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the area brought heavy snow to the South Washington Cascades. Snow along the front and post frontal showers brought 12-20 inches of snow to multiple locations around 4000 feet and higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple locations around 4000' and above recorded 12-20 of snow.  Highest amounts observed were recorded at Pinto Rock SNOTEL at 4440' elevation and Lone Pine SNOTEL at 3930' elevation, which both recorded 20 inches of snow.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A post-frontal Puget Sound convergence zone brought heavy snow to Stampede Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stevens Pass NWAC site recorded 11 inches of snow in a 9-hour period ending at 2 am Wed Nov 16. WashDOT reported 12 inches new snow to NWAC by phone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was brief high wind in a few zones of the northwest interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lopez island recorded 46g55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was brief high wind in a few zones of the northwest interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bellingham CWOP recorded 40g59 mph.  Sandy Point Shores recorded 39g62 mph.  Ferndale CWOP recorded 32g66 mph.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system brought heavy snow to the North Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mt Baker NWAC site measured 13 inches snow in the 12 hours ending 1 am Monday November 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific front moving slowly across the area produced heavy rainfall which caused local river flooding along the Grays River near Rosburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flooding of the Grays River near Rosburg. Grays River reached flood stage at 1021 AM on November 24th, and crested at 13.9 feet at 1215 AM November 25th. The river dropped below flood stage at 850 AM November 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist pacific storm system brought heavy snow to the mountains of Northeast Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported 6 inches of snow 4 miles west of Boyds, Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist pacific storm system brought heavy snow to the mountains of Northeast Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ski area near Stevens Pass, Washington reported between 7 and 11 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist pacific storm system brought heavy snow to the mountains of Northeast Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mount Spokane Ski and Snowboard park, located 23 miles northeast of Spokane, reported 8.5 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking northeastward off the Coast brought high winds to the South Washington Coast, with sustained winds up to 45 mph observed at the Astoria/Megler bridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on the Astoria/Megler Bridge recorded sustained winds up to 45 mph.
